does: This paper identifies and articulates fundamental vagueness in how AI safety and ethical behavior are defined and evaluated in high-stakes social contexts. Rather than treating vagueness as a purely technical problem resolvable through mathematical formalism, the authors reframe it as a sociotechnical challenge requiring deliberation about political dimensions and deployment contexts. They introduce the Hard Choices in AI (HCAI) framework, which maps critical decision points across AI system development stages and connects each to major sociotechnical challenges. The framework serves two concrete functions: it identifies overlaps between specific design choices and broader safety concerns, and it structures the creation of stakeholder feedback mechanisms to ensure comprehensive safety issue identification and resolution.
connects: This work builds on growing literature examining AI safety beyond narrow technical metrics, extending critiques of purely mathematical approaches to ethics and safety developed in algorithmic auditing and value alignment research. It responds to foundational debates in AI ethics about whose values are embedded in systems and how diverse stakeholder perspectives should be integrated, drawing connections to participatory design and responsible innovation frameworks. The paper engages with sociotechnical systems analysis traditions that reject technology determinism, joining recent work questioning whether formal methods alone can capture real-world deployment complexities. It particularly connects to research on contextual integrity and situated ethics that emphasizes how safety meanings shift across different social domains and stakeholder positions.
enables: This framework creates pathways for more democratic and accountable AI development by establishing structured processes for stakeholder deliberation at critical junctures. It enables practitioners to systematically identify which design decisions carry implications for multiple stakeholder groups and social values, rather than treating safety as a post-hoc compliance concern. The HCAI approach points toward institutional innovations in AI governance, particularly mechanisms for continuous stakeholder feedback throughout development rather than one-time ethical review. It sets up future work examining how to operationalize stakeholder engagement at scale, how to resolve conflicts between stakeholders with competing values, and how to maintain deliberative processes as systems scale. The framework potentially strengthens democratic governance of AI by making technical development processes more transparent and contestable.
